coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional silicon quantum dots sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of p-dihydroxybenzene with double signals
coredata.dc:creator: Yi, Yinhui
Liu, Lirong
Zeng, Wei
Lv, Baohe
Zhu, Gangbing
coredata.dc:description: Being awfully harmful to the environment and human health, the sensitive and selective determination of p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB) in environmental water is of great significance. However, the similar structures and properties of p-DHB with catechol (CC; one p-DHB isomer) make the selective and reliable determination of p-DHB from CC in environmental waters being still a formidable challenge. In this manuscript, by preparing CC stabilized silicon quantum dots (C-SiQDs) via a simple hydrothermal process and using C-SiQDs simultaneously as a catalyst and fluorescence indicator, a novel nanosensing platform was successfully developed to selectively and sensitively detect p-DHB with double signals: fluorescence and colorimetric. For detecting p-DHB, C-SiQDs could catalyze the oxidation of p-DHB to form benzoquinone (BQ), an intermediate, which lead to the color change from pale yellow to dark brown and the efficient quenching the fluorescence of C-SiQDs. While for CC in samples, it could not contact C-SiQDs surface due to the great steric hindrance effect from CC on quantum dots surface, thus restricting the oxidation of CC and production of BQ, and leading that the obtained C-SiQDs have no response towards CC. Meanwhile, the developed nanosensor could also avoid various other possible interferences. Under optimized conditions, wide linear ranges (from 0.01 μM to 50.0 μM) and low detection limits (4.0 nM) were obtained for p-DHB. The simple and reliable C-SiQDs-based double signals proposal demonstrated to be potentially applicable for the highly selective and sensitive detection of trace p-DHB in various environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: p-Dihydroxybenzene
Catechol
Silicon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The catalytic activity of C-SiQDs and their highly selective response to p-DHB
Sensitivity of C-SiQDs for p-DHB detection
Selectivity of C-SiQDs for p-DHB detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) EDS pattern of the C-SiQDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of C-SiQDs (black), C-SiQDs with p-DHB (red), and C-SiQDs in the presence of hydroquinone and glutathione (blue); (b) the absorbance of C-SiQDs (black), p-DHB (red), and C-SiQDs with p-DHB (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of C-SiQDs (black), C-SiQDs with 40.0 μmol/L CC (red), C-SiQDs with 40.0 μmol/L p-DHB (blue). (b) The absorbance of C-SiQDs (black), C-SiQDs with 40.0 μmol/L CC (red), and C-SiQDs with 40.0 μmol/L p-DHB (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Quenching effects of p-DHB or CC on C-SiQDs or (APTMS)-SiQDs' fluorescence in pH 8.0 PBS buffer solution. (b) UV–visible absorbance spectra of C-SiQDs, mixture of C-SiQDs and 40.0 μM catechol, mixture of C-SiQDs and 40.0 μM p-DHB, (APTMS)-SiQDs, mixture of (APTMS)SiQDs and 40.0 μM CC and mixture of (APTMS)-SiQDs and 40.0 μM p-DHB.

(a) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of C-SiQDs and the concentration of p-DHB; (b) the calibration curves for the detection of p-DHB. (c) the relationship between the absorbance of C-SiQDs and the concentration of p-DHB; (d) the calibration curves for the detection of p-DHB. Inset in (d) shows the digital images of change in color of C-SiQDs with addition of p-DHB.
Selectivity analysis for p-DHB detection by the quenched fluorescence intensities of C-SiQDs. Fluorescence responses of C-SiQDs with different interferences.
(a) Synthesis strategy of the C-SiQDs using DAMO and CC by a hydrothermal method; (b) schematic illustration of p-DHB detection principle based on the nanoprobe of bifunctional C-SiQDs.
Comparison of the determination for p-DHB using different methods.
Analysis of p-DHB in water samples.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional silicon quantum dots sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of p-dihydroxybenzene with double signals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Being awfully harmful to the environment and human health, the sensitive and selective determination of p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB) in environmental water is of great significance. However, the similar structures and properties of p-DHB with catechol (CC; one p-DHB isomer) make the selective and reliable determination of p-DHB from CC in environmental waters being still a formidable challenge. In this manuscript, by preparing CC stabilized silicon quantum dots (C-SiQDs) via a simple hydrothermal process and using C-SiQDs simultaneously as a catalyst and fluorescence indicator, a novel nanosensing platform was successfully developed to selectively and sensitively detect p-DHB with double signals: fluorescence and colorimetric. For detecting p-DHB, C-SiQDs could catalyze the oxidation of p-DHB to form benzoquinone (BQ), an intermediate, which lead to the color change from pale yellow to dark brown and the efficient quenching the fluorescence of C-SiQDs. While for CC in samples, it could not contact C-SiQDs surface due to the great steric hindrance effect from CC on quantum dots surface, thus restricting the oxidation of CC and production of BQ, and leading that the obtained C-SiQDs have no response towards CC. Meanwhile, the developed nanosensor could also avoid various other possible interferences. Under optimized conditions, wide linear ranges (from 0.01 μM to 50.0 μM) and low detection limits (4.0 nM) were obtained for p-DHB. The simple and reliable C-SiQDs-based double signals proposal demonstrated to be potentially applicable for the highly selective and sensitive detection of trace p-DHB in various environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: A sort of phenol compounds, p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB), plays an essential role in lots of industrial and biological possesses such as photographic developers, coal-tar production and paper manufacturing [1–3]. Meanwhile, p-DHB is also a major component of pollutants produced by agricultural actives and industrial wastewater [4,5]. Moreover, p-DHB has extremely detrimental influences on humans through oral, dermal and respiratory tracks, which results in serious health hazards and environmental problems [6–8]. Considering of the harmful effect and persistence of p-DHB in environment water, developing sensitive and selective methods for p-DHB detection is urgent.

Up to now, significant efforts have been devoted for the sensitive and rapid determination of p-DHB. Among these methods, the peroxidase/H2O2 system has attracted extensive interest in the areas of the sensing of p-DHB, as peroxidase can catalyze the oxidation of p-DHB in the presence of H2O2 to produce benzoquinone (BQ) intermediates [9]. For example, Huang et al. [10] utilized water-soluble fluorescent conjugated polymer-enzyme hybrid system to detect p-DHB; Li et al. [11] constructed a graphene quantum dots-enzyme hybrid system for the determination of p-DHB. However, the intrinsic limitations of enzyme, such as leakage, denaturation and high cost of preparation and purification limit their applications. Thus, it is important to develop nonenzymatic fluorescent methods for the determination of p-DHB. Recently, graphene quantum dots [12], carbon dots [13] and conjugated polymer nanoparticles [14] have been reported for nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing of p-DHB, which provides great promise to develop facile non-enzyme sensors of p-DHB. However, these methods only possess a single signal for the determination of p-DHB, which may be not reliable enough since some factors (such as detection surroundings and mistakes) could interfere with the detection results [15,16], while sensing platform with multiple signals could make the determination results more convincing. Especially, these methods cannot selectively distinguish p-DHB from catechol (CC) owing to the similar structures and properties of p-DHB and CC, leading to cause great interference in sensing p-DHB since CC generally coexist in the same water system with p-DHB. Therefore, developing high selective non-enzyme nanosensors to identify p-DHB from CC with multiple signals is very important.

Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), as a new type of abundant and low-cost fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted much interest and have been demonstrated to be environmentally friendly fluorescent probes. Furthermore, compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, SiQDs are superior in terms of cytotoxicity, photostability, water solubility, and photoluminescence profiles. These interesting advantages enable SiQDs to play a great role in a variety of fields such as bioimaging, biosensors and biological labeling [17–22]. For instance, Ma et al. [23] reported a water-dispersible and biocompatible SiQDs for selective heparin sensing and cell imaging. Zhang et al. [24] synthesized a highly photoluminescent SiQDs nanoprobe for the detection of dopamine. Recently, we have also developed SiQDs-based fluorescent sensing assay for the detection of glucose [25] and organophosphorus pesticides [26]. In addition to the above properties, SiQDs have been proved to possess highly-efficient peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activity [27]. These unique features of SiQDs can be highly exploitable in the development of novel optical sensors.

In this work, by using SiQDs reduced and stabilized by CC (C-SiQDs) as a catalyst and fluorescence indicator, we designed a simple, reliable, sensitive and selective sensing system to distinguish p-DHB from CC with double signals including fluorescence and colorimetric (Scheme 1 ). In this sensing system, when C-SiQDs and p-DHB solution were mixed simply, C-SiQDs could catalyze the oxidation of p-DHB to form BQ due to the peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activity of C-SiQDs, which resulted that the color of the solution changed from pale yellow to dark brown, and the generated BQ could simultaneously quench fluorescence of C-SiQDs. On the basis of the changes in fluorescence and color, a highly selective and sensitive nanosensor platform with double signals could be obtained for sensing p-DHB by using C-SiQDs. While for CC, it could not contact C-SiQDs surface resulted from the great steric hindrance effect from CC on C-SiQDs surface, thus the oxidation of CC and production of BQ could not be achieved, making the obtained C-SiQDs-based nanosensor could successfully identify p-DHB from CC. Moreover, various real water samples were used to demonstrate the applications of the developed nanosensor, the experimental results suggested that the recoveries are satisfactory. It's believed that this work is of great significance for the selective and sensitive determination of p-DHB in real applications and developing novel SiQDs-based nanosensors.

N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO), p-DHB, CC, sodium citrate, resorcinol, dopamine, (3-Aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS), phenol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other regents of analytical grade were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used as received. All reagents are of analytical grade and used without any further purification. All solutions were prepared using deionized water in experimental procedures.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-7000 Spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology and structure of C-SiQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-3010, Joel, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, NICOLET 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Tecnai G2 20) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha 1063,Thermo Fisher Co., UK). The pH measurements were performed with a pH-meter PB-10 (Sartorius, Germany). The fluorescence spectra and absorbance of the resulting solutions were recorded at room temperature (25 °C).

C-SiQDs were synthesized by an oxidation-reduction process with DAMO as silicon source and CC as reductant via a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1a). In brief, after adding 2.0 mL of DAMO to 10.0 mL of deionized water with stirring, 11.0 mg of CC was added to the above solution by stirring for 1 min. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and processed under 200 °C for 4 h. Finally, the as-prepared C-SiQDs were cooled to room temperature and purified through a dialysis tube (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 6 h. The obtained C-SiQDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The synthetic procedure of (APTMS)-SiQDs has been previously reported by Zhang et al. [18]. In a typical experiment, 1.0 g of sodium citrate was dissolved in 30 mL of water, followed by oxygen removal through purging with nitrogen gas of high purity for about 30 min. 1.0 mL of APTMS was added and homogeneously agitated, afterwards, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 50.0 mL volume, which was sealed and maintained at a temperature of 160 °C for 12 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting transparent solution was dialyzed against stirred water in a dialysis bag with a 1 kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6 h before precipitate occurred in the bag. Finally, the solution in the bag was collected and stored at 4 °C for use.

The obtained C-SiQDs were diluted 100-fold with 10.0 mM PBS buffer solution (pH = 8.0) for the fluorescence and absorbance measurements. Then a certain volume of the p-DHB with different concentrations (0.01 to 50.0 μM) was mixed with the as-prepared C-SiQDs solution in the media of 10.0 mM PBS of pH 8.0 at room temperature. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity were measured after 10 min. Each experiment was repeated 3 times. The detection procedures for other phenolic compounds and interferences were similar to p-DHB.

Tap water (from Lab), lake water (Yudai Lake, Jiangsu University), rainwater samples (collected during rain in Zhenjiang city a period from 10 to 13 May 2018,) were used. These collected samples were centrifuged and the supernatant was diluted 10 times with 10.0 mM PBS buffer solution (pH 8.0). The diluted water samples were added with different concentrations of p-DHB (0.5 mM, 5.0 mM and 30.0 mM) to prepare the spiked samples. Real samples detection was carried out using the procedure described above.

The morphologies of the obtained C-SiQDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1a). It's noted that the produced C-SiQDs were uniform in size with an average diameter of 3.3 nm. The EDS pattern of C-SiQDs indicated that C-SiQDs contain Si, C, O, and N elements (Fig. 1b). FT-IR was used to perform the presence of various functional groups in C-SiQDs (Fig. S1f), and the absorption peaks at 3371 and 3290 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH from CC and NH [28] respectively, while the relatively strong peak at 1635 cm−1 could be ascribed to the bending vibration of NH [29]. Meanwhile, the broad peak at 777 cm−1 suggested the existence of NH wagging vibration [30]. Moreover, the secondary amine exhibited a peak at 696 cm-1, and a peak belonging to SiO stretching was at 923 cm−1 [30]. These results demonstrated that the C-SiQDs contains amount of hydroxyl and amino groups which could greatly enhance solubility and stability of the C-SiQDs.

XPS was further used to perform the surface composition for the obtained C-SiQDs (Fig. S1). It's noted from Fig. S1a that there are five major peaks present at 101.3, 152.1, 283.7, 399.1, and 532.1 eV which corresponded to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S1c) exhibited three fitted peaks which were ascribed to NSi (398.2 eV), CNC (399.1 eV), and N(C)3 (400.2 eV). Two peaks at 530.6 and 531.8 eV were attributed to SiO in the XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. S1d), and the peak at 532.3 eV corresponded to CO [31]. The Si 2p spectrum (Fig. S1e) exhibited three peaks at 101.1, 101.7, and 102.4 eV, which were associated with SiC, SiN and SiO groups, respectively [32].

Fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were used to study the optical properties of the synthesized C-SiQDs. As seen from Fig. S2a, the C-SiQDs aqueous solution shows obvious typical UV–Vis absorbance band at 430 nm comparing to DAMO and CC. And the C-SiQDs solution was yellowish, transparent and clear under sunlight; meanwhile, it could emit bright green light under UV light excitation (inset in Fig. S2b), suggesting that the obtained C-SiQDs have excellent fluorescence property. Additionally, the as-prepared fluorescent SiNPs emitted a green fluorescence with a highly quantum yield of about 7.1% [33]. As shown in Fig. S2b, the fluorescence emission spectra of C-SiQDs exhibited a typical excitation-dependent feature and the emission peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength, with an optimum excitation at 430 nm and emission maximum at 520 nm. Besides, the fluorescence of C-SiQDs showed remarkable stability. It was noted from Fig. S3a that the normalized fluorescence almost did not change when the pH values ranged from 4 to 12, indicating that the C-SiQDs has excellent stability in acid and alkali. As such, the normalized fluorescence of C-SiQDs did not change obviously under continuous irradiation (Fig. S3b), suggesting that its photostability is very outstanding. In addition, the obtained C-SiQDs solution could keep stable for several months (Fig. S3c), and the normalized fluorescence changed less than 5% in NaCl solution with high concentration (1.5 M) (Fig. S3d). These results demonstrated that the C-SiQDs are excellent candidates as a powerful reagent and probe in biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalytic activity of C-SiQDs and their highly selective response to p-DHB
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of sensing p-DHB with double signals by using C-SiQDs were first investigated thoroughly. The fluorescence response of C-SiQDs was tested under different conditions. It's noted from Fig. 2a that a large inhibition extent was observed when C-SiQDs and p-DHB were simply mixed. Based on previous studies [9,10], BQ, including 1,2-BQ and 1,4-BQ, was assumed to be a fluorescent quencher for C-SiQDs. Therefore, we then add GSH to the mixed solution of C-SiQDs and p-DHB. The fluorescence of the C-SiQDs was recovered, indicating that BQ was reduced to p-DHB. Additionally, the TEM assays of SiQDs treated with p-DHB were performed. As shown in Fig. S4a and b, nanoparticles with a particle size of 100 nm appear and most of them are polymerized together. SiQDs are distributed in and around the nanoparticles (Fig. S4c). It is indicated that the BQ was generated and might be polymerized together. Furthermore, we tested the zeta potential of p-DHB, C-SiQDs and mixed solutions of p-DHB and C-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S5, the zeta potential of p-DHB solution is about −13.6 mV, and the zeta potential of C-SiQDs solution is about 6.9 mV. This indicates that the negatively charged p-DHB could contacts the positively charged C-SiQDs by electrostatic force. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of the mixed solution of p-DHB and C-SiQDs is about −2.5 mV. This low potential indicates that the mixed solution of C-SiQDs and BQ generated by p-DHB oxidation is unstable and tends to aggregate. Zeta potential test results are fully consistent with TEM test results. In order to further explore the quenching mechanism, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of C-SiQDs before and after treated with p-DHB was tested. As shown in Fig. S6, there were no obvious changes of fluorescence lifetime among C-SiQDs before and after treated with p-DHB. Furthermore, after testing for UV absorption of C-SiQDs before and after treated with p-DHB, the change of the absorption spectrum of C-SiQD before and after the addition of p-DHB was observed (Fig. 2b). Therefore, based on the above results, fluorescence quenching can be described as static quenching.

Since C-SiQDs is the only reagent used in this system, we speculated that C-SiQDs could catalyze the oxidation of p-DHB by dissolved oxygen to form BQ which can quench the fluorescence of C-SiQDs. To demonstrate our hypothesis, we investigated the effect of dissolved oxygen. As shown in Fig. S7a, the fluorescence of C-SiQDs changed little in N2-saturated C-SiQDs aqueous solution containing p-DHB. While in the presence of dissolved oxygen, the p-DHB can remarkably quench the fluorescence of C-SiQDs, which indicate that the dissolved oxygen is one of the most essential factor in the sensing system. Then, the effect of catalytic activity of C-SiQDs was investigated. It was noted from Fig. S7b that colorless p-DHB solution can be observed in alkaline medium. Upon adding C-SiQDs to p-DHB solution, the color system was changed and a brown color was observed. The color of mixture of p-DHB and C-SiQDs becomes darker brown with the increase of p-DHB concentration. The experiments indicated that the reaction of p-DHB and dissolved oxygen can be accelerated by the addition of C-SiQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of two p-DHB solution (10.0 μM and 40.0 μM) as well as their mixture with C-SiQDs are depicted in Fig. S7c. The absorption intensity in whole spectrum of the mixture of p-DHB and C-SiQDs greatly increases compared to the absorption intensity of free p-DHB, and the absorption intensity increases with the increase of p-DHB concentration. These results indicated that C-SiQDs has excellent catalytic activity. Therefore, based on the catalysis of C-SiQDs, p-DHB can be transformed to BQ, a very effective quencher, which can remarkably quench the fluorescence of C-SiQDs.

Up to now, it is very difficult to use nanozyme probes to distinguish selectively p-DHB from CC. In our experiments, it was interesting to find that C-SiQDs probes have good selectivity for p-DHB and CC; that's to say, CC shows no interface for sensing p-DHB (Fig. 3 ). Considering that the C-SiQDs is formed by the reaction of CC as a reducing agent and stabilizer with DAMO, and the SiH bond on the surface of C-SiQDs plays a key role in the catalytic activity of C-SiQDs [34], we suspect that CC on the surface of C-SiQDs can prevent CC in the solution from contacting C-SiQDs because of the greater steric hindrance effect, which could restrict CC to be oxidized and form BQ, thus the fluorescence of C-SiQDs cannot be quenched and the C-SiQDs solution color would not be changed. In order to prove this hypothesis, the steric effect of C-SiQDs towards CC was studied. As shown in Fig. 4a, The fluorescence of (APTMS)-SiQDs is obviously quenched by p-DHB and CC. However, the fluorescence of C-SiQDs is only significant quenched by p-DHB. At the same time, it can be seen from the digital picture in Fig. 4a that only adding p-DHB to the C-SiQDs solution, the color of the solution become dark brown significantly, but there was little change after the addition of CC. UV–vis absorption spectra for C-SiQDs and (APTMS)-SiQDs as well as their respective mixed solutions of p-DHB and CC are shown in Fig. 4b. The whole absorption spectrum of (APTMS)-SiQDs added with p-DHB and CC respectively was significantly enhanced compared with the whole absorption spectrum of (APTMS)-SiQDs. The absorbance of C-SiQDs solution with p-DHB is strongly enhanced compared with the absorbance of C-SiQDs, while the absorbance of C-SiQDs increased little after the addition of CC due to the low reaction rate of CC and dissolved oxygen in alkaline medium. These results demonstrated that CC on the surface of C-SiQDs largely prevents the CC in solution from contacting C-SiQDs and thus it cannot be catalyzed to form BQ, nor can it quench the fluorescence of C-SiQDs.

In order to have a better response to the fluorescence probe for sensing p-DHB, we optimized the conditions including pH value, concentrations of C-SiQDs and reacting time. As shown in Fig. S8a and b, the fluorescence activity of C-SiQDs is almost unaffected by altering the pH of solution, and the quenching effect of p-DHB on the fluorescence intensity of C-SiQDs increases with increasing pH value, and tended to reach a maximum value at 8 (where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities at 520 nm in the presence and absence of p-DHB, respectively). Therefore, the pH 8.0 was adopted in the subsequent work. Another key factor for the sensing system is the concentration of C-SiQDs. The effect of different amounts of C-SiQDs was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence ratio (F/F0). As indicated in Fig. S8(c–d), the final selection of 100-fold diluted C-SiQDs solution was the best fluorescence ratio. To understand the response rate of the fluorescence signal of the C-SiQDs to p-DHB, the changes of time-dependent fluorescence were measured in the presence of p-DHB. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of C-SiQDs solution decreased with the incubation time from 0 to 10 min. When incubation time was longer than 10 min, the quenching effect reached a plain, and incubation time of 10 min was adopted for the subsequent fluorescent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of C-SiQDs for p-DHB detection
ce:section.ce:para: By using C-SiQDs as the catalyst and the fluorescence probe, the linear response range of the sensing system was measured under the optimized conditions discussed above. It was noted from Fig. 5(a–b) that the fluorescence peak of C-SiQDs at 520 nm decreased gradually upon stepwise addition of p-DHB, and it shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of C-SiQDs (520 nm) and the concentration of p-DHB from 0.01 to 50.0 μM (R2 = 0.998), and the limit of detection (LOD) for p-DHB is 4.0 nM (S/N = 3). As such, it can be seen in Fig. 5c that the UV–vis absorbance peak near 440 nm increased when the p-DHB increased from 0.01 to 50.0 μM with a slight red-shift, indicating that p-DHB was transformed to BQ with the catalysis of C-SiQDs. Fig. 5d shows a good linear relationship between increased absorbance intensity (440 nm) and the concentration of p-DHB when the p-DHB increased from 0.01 to 50.0 μM (R2 = 0.992, the LOD of p-DHB was 5.0 nM). Inset in Fig. 5d is the digital picture of C-SiQDs in the presence of p-DHB with different concentrations, suggesting that the color change caused by the oxidation of p-DHB to BQ was obvious. Moreover, a comparison between other reported methods and the proposed method for p-DHB determination was summarized in Table 1 . It was noted that the developed sensing system in this work reveals remarkable sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of C-SiQDs for p-DHB detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed sensor for p-DHB was then investigated by analyzing various possible interferences including ions (Na+, K+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cl−, NO3 −, HPO4 2−, and H2PO4−), p-DHB-similar species (CC, resorcinol, phenol, and dopamine), and reducing organisms (uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, glutathione, cysteine, glycine and homocysteine). As seen from Fig. 6 , these interfering substances have nearly no interference to the fluorescence response of 40.0 μM p-DHB even at a rather high concentration (Na+, K+, Cl− and NO3 − were 5 mM. The others were 0.05 mM). The results obviously indicated that the developed method exhibited excellent selectivity for the detection of p-DHB.

To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method for detection of p-DHB in real environmental water samples, three kinds of water samples (lake water, rainwater and tap-water) were investigated in this study. Meanwhile, standard addition experiments were performed to detect p-DHB. As shown in Table 2 , it shows that the recovery values are in the range of 95.2%–106.1%. These results indicated that the proposed method has potential applications in real sample analysis for p-DHB. Furthermore, to prove the accuracy of this method, these samples were also tested by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The results obtained by HPLC and fluorescence and colorimetric method based on C-SiQDs are in good agreement, indicating that this method is satisfactory (Table 2).

In summary, by preparing C-SiQDs simultaneously as a catalyst and a fluorescence indicator, a novel reliable non-enzyme nanosensing platform was constructed for sensing p-DHB with fluorescence and colorimetry double signals. The results show that the developed nanosensing platform could successfully selectively detect p-DHB from CC and other possible interferences. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed C-SiQDs-based sensor has a wide linger range from 0.01 μM to 50.0 μM with a low LOD of 4.0 nM for p-DHB analysis, and the nanosensing platform was also applied successfully to detect p-DHB in various environmental water samples including tap water, lake water, and rainwater. Actually, this work is the first time to introduce a C-SiQDs sensor coupled with double responses for the determination of p-DHB. It's confirmed that the proposed nanosensor, with its simplicity and excellent selectivity and sensitivity, has great potential applications to detect p-DHB in the environmental monitoring and controlling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21607061), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (2018019), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Material of Water Treatment, and the Program of Young Backbone Teachers in Jiangsu University (2015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.03.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of a quantum dot-based molecular imprinting nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of 4-nitrophenol in environmental waters
